EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough over the Islands held  the potential for flash flooding for 02/02/2022 to 02/03/2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a 24-hour period, WSO Pago Pago recorded 4.02 inches of rain, which prompted a flash flood warning for the Islands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough that remained anchored over the territory through the timeframe of events produce heavy rainfall for the Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EOC reported a landslide near Agugulu enroute WSO Pago Pago. Near two inches of rain in a 24 hour period was recorded at WSO Pago Paog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy conditions and building swells produced rough sea-conditions for mariners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO Pago Pago recorded 18 knots at the most for this duration, while ASCAT pass observed 25 knots. These winds created rough conditions for mariners that capsized a vessel east of Aunu'u Island around 4:09 AM. see article https://www.talanei.com/2022/05/23/four-on-board-capsized-alia-escape-unharmed/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of wind and south swells produced surfs of 9 to 12 feet across south shores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong surfs and dangerous rip currents swept 22-year-old Niutea Laulu of Vailoa at Leala Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced heavy showers for the territory that produced flash flooding across Tutuila on the 26th and 27th of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flooding in poor drainage areas such as cost u less and fagaima road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced heavy showers for the territory that produced flash flooding across Tutuila on the 26th and 27th of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The national weather service recorded 5.29 inches of rain within a 24-hour period due to a stationary front. This amount of rainfall produced widespread flash flooding across Tutuila.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter low anchored near New Zealand produced large record breaking swells that greatly impacted American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sea level rose and high waves crashed onto the roads for this timeframe, mainly south and east facing shores. A national declaration was declared for American Samoa due to the widespread damage from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Pacific Convergence zone (SPCZ) moving across the territory resulted in heavy rainfall over the Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rain gauge at the local weather office reported 3.46 inches of rain within this 36 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The SPCZ produced heavy rainfall across the territory, mainly in central and western villages on Tutuila.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain observations by Meteorologist Joe Laplante at Coconut Point recorded (tipping bucket by Davis Instruments): 4.27 inches between 1 AM and 2 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The SPCZ produced heavy rainfall across the territory, mainly in central and western villages on Tutuila.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASCC (tipping bucket by Davis Instruments): 2.49 inches between 1 AM and 2 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The SPCZ produced heavy rainfall across the territory, mainly in central and western villages on Tutuila.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO Pago Pago recorded 2.58 inches between 12:50 AM and 1:50 AM, while at the same time several reports were received of flooding in low-lying areas. Also a power outage in Fagaitua was also reported during the time of this flash flooding event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Pacific Convergence zone (SPCZ) moving across the territory resulted in heavy rainfall over the Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee Joe Laplnate from Coconut Point reported (tipping bucket by Davis Instruments) 1.27 inches from 8:29 to 8:59 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The volcanic eruption in Tonga caused tsunami waves to reach American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EOC reported tsunami inundation in the harbor area due to volcanic eruption. Wave heights of 2.0 feet were recorded there at 6:31 PM SST. Coast erosion and inundation were observed in low-lying coastal areas. Mainly Onenoa reported large debris on roads, making it difficult for driving.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Pacific Convergence zone (SPCZ) moving across the territory resulted in heavy rainfall over the Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Meteorologist Joe Laplante recorded 1.05 inches of rain at his person rain gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough that remained anchored over the territory through the timeframe of events produce heavy rainfall for the Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saturated grounds due to heavy rainfall were reported from across the territory, including 3.20 inches of rain at the local weather office. This event resulted in a landslide in Nuuuli two days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large southerly swells produced dangerous rip currents and high surfs for the territory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents swept out at sea two village boys from Amanave. Thankfully the boys had a surf board with them, which made it possible for rescue within a two-hour window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge nearly stationary over the territory for most of March produced a gentle breeze and limited shower development for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the following dates, WSO Pago Pago broke record high temperatures or tied due to limited showers in the area: ||March 12 - Tied Record > 92 Degrees F|March 15 - Broke Record > 93 Degrees F |March 17 - Broke Record > 94 Degrees F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge nearly stationary over the territory for most of March produced a gentle breeze and limited shower development for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the following dates, WSO Pago Pago broke record high temperatures or tied due to limited showers in the area: ||March 4 - Tied Record > 92 Degrees F |March 5 - Broke Record > 93 Degrees F|March 7 - Tied Record > 94 Degrees F |March 8 - Tied Record > 92 Degrees F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active SPCZ located just south of the islands produced heavy rainfall, which led to flash flooding  for American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in drainages, streams, roads, properties and other low-lying areas were reported in the Tualauta District. Landslides were reported in the village of Fagaalu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge nearly stationary over the territory for most of March produced a gentle breeze and limited shower development for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the following dates, WSO Pago Pago broke record high temperatures or tied due to limited showers in the area: March 12 - Tied Record > 91 Degrees F March 23 - Tied > 91 Degrees F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced heavy showers and flash flooding on May 13 and May 15 to May 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO Pago Pago recorded 1.79 inches of rain in the last 24 hours. Flash flooding was reported in poor drainage areas such as Tafuna county by Cost u less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced heavy showers and flash flooding on May 13 and May 15 to May 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO reported 4.27 inches of rain during this 48 hour period. Reports of rockslide in ofu, landslide near Agugulu road, and overflowing waterfall near main road along the village of Failolo were also received during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter low anchored near New Zealand produced large record breaking swells that greatly impacted American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sea level rose and high waves crashed onto the roads for this timeframe, mainly south and east facing shores. The high-impacted villages include: Fatu ma Futi, Aunu'u, Alega, Laulii, and Tula. These villages experienced damages to roads, road closure, and debris covering roadways. A national declaration was declared for American Samoa due to the widespread damage from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved over American Samoa to give rainfall over all islands of the territory. WSO Pago Pago recorded 2.19 inches on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall likely caused a landslide near the village of Asili. The landslide cause multiple downed trees and debris over the main road. There were no injuries.
